en.extract:
The pink-headed warbler (Cardellina versicolor) is a small passerine bird found in the southwestern highlands of Guatemala and the central and southeastern highlands of the Mexican state of Chiapas.  The adult is primarily red, with a silvery-pink head and chest.  It is a fairly common to common resident of humid to semi-humid pine-oak, pine-evergreen and evergreen forest and edge, at altitudes ranging from 1,800–3,500 m (5,900–11,500 ft) above sea level.


Taxonomy
When Osbert Salvin first described the pink-headed warbler in 1864, he assigned it to the genus Cardellina.  It was also briefly assigned to Setophaga, the genus of the American redstart, before being moved to the genus Ergaticus in 1881. It is monotypic across its limited range, but forms a superspecies with the red warbler, which is found in the highlands of Mexico, north of the Isthmus of Tehuantepec.  Despite disjunct populations and considerably different plumages, the two have sometimes been considered to be conspecific.
A comprehensive 2010 paper by Irby Lovette and colleagues analysing mitochondrial and nuclear DNA of the wood-warblers found that the red and pink-headed warblers were each other's closest relative and that their common ancestor diverged from a lineage that gave rise to the red-faced warbler. The authors recommended moving the red and pink-headed warblers back to the genus Cardellina, which has been adopted by the International Ornithologists' Union (IOC).
"Pink-headed warbler" has been designated the official name by the IOC, and is a reference to its most notable feature. The genus name Cardellina is the diminutive of the Italian cardella, a regional name for the European goldfinch, while its specific name, versicolor, is Latin for 'of changeable or various colors'. The latter is a reference to the warbler's changeable head color, which, depending on the angle of the viewer, looks either frosty pink or a deeper red than the rest of its body. In Spanish, the species is called cabeza plateada or 'silvery head'.


Description

The pink-headed warbler measures 12.5–13.5 cm (4.9–5.3 in) in length, and weighs 10 g (0.35 oz).  Both sexes have a similar plumage, though females are, on average, slightly duller overall. The adult has dark red upperparts, a silvery-pink chest and pinkish-red underparts.  Its head is silvery-pink, with a reddish forehead, dusky lores and dark brown irises.  Its bill is blackish, sometimes showing some horn color on the lower mandible, and its legs are flesh-colored.
The juvenile is a rich brown with slightly paler underparts.  However, that plumage is quickly molted. By late summer, young birds are virtually indistinguishable from adults; only their unossified skulls distinguish them.


Voice
The pink-headed warbler's call is high, thin, and somewhat metallic, variously transcribed as tsiu, ssing or tseeip. It also has a low, weak chip that it uses to stay in contact with its mate. Its song is a series of short trills and chips, described as being "clear and cheery" and reminiscent of the song of the yellow warbler, as well as slower and less varied than that of the red warbler.  Only males sing. While its calls are heard year-round, the pink-headed warbler sings mostly between February and May, and is silent during the rainy month of June.


Distribution and habitat
The pink-headed warbler is endemic to the highlands of central and eastern Chiapas in Mexico, and to western Guatemala.  It is a fairly common to common resident of humid to semi-humid pine-oak, pine-evergreen and evergreen forest and edge, at altitudes ranging from 1,800–3,500 m (5,900–11,500 ft) above sea level.  It prefers forest with dense, undisturbed understory, but is also found in disturbed forest with damaged understory in its strongholds in Guatemala; this is thought to be suboptimal habitat. Though it is among the species expected to occur in El Salvador, it has not yet been reported there.


Behavior
Except when they are feeding fledged young, it is uncommon to find more than two pink-headed warblers together.  Mated pairs typically remain together year-round.


Food and feeding
Like other New World warblers, the pink-headed warbler is an insectivore, gleaning insects and other invertebrates from vegetation (primarily in dense understory) and making aerial sallies after flying prey. It typically forages between 2–5 m (6.6–16.4 ft) off the ground, only seldom foraging above 7 m (23 ft) — except during the breeding season, when the male may hunt near the tops of trees from which it sings, as high as 15 m (49 ft) up. The species often joins mixed species flocks that pass through its territory. There is some evidence that volcanic eruptions, which can coat vegetation with thick ash and impact insect populations, cause declines in pink-headed warbler numbers.


Breeding
In the spring, the male begins singing on good weather days in early February and continues for the next several months, with song frequency peaking between March and May.  The female makes a globular nest from pine needles, building it on the ground in an area with a significant layer of fallen pine needles, often on a steep bank.  She collects pine needles more than 50 ft (15 m) away from the nest's location, checking carefully for potential predators before returning to the nest site.  Once the exterior is completed, she fills the bottom of the nest with soft fibrous materials and lines it with moss.  She lays 2–4 eggs, which are white with a wreath of pale brown spots at the large end and a sparse speckling of pale brown spots elsewhere.  The eggs average 17.1 mm × 13.3 mm (0.67 in × 0.52 in) in size. The female alone incubates for 16 days, sitting within the domed nest with her tail sticking out of the opening and her head turned so she can see out.  She is restless while incubating, regularly changing her position.  All told, she spends some 71 percent of her time on the nest during incubation, in stints of 13–35 minutes (average 20.1 minutes) with breaks of  4–13 minutes (average 8.3 minutes). Unlike many ground-nesting species, the pink-headed warbler has no distraction display.
The young pink-headed warbler has a red mouth lining, a feature which has been used by taxonomists working to unravel warbler systematics. Nestlings spend some 10–12 days in the nest before fledging.  They begin pair bonding by their first autumn.


Conservation and threats
Across its range, pink-headed warbler numbers are declining, primarily because the cloud forest upon which it depends is becoming increasingly fragmented. However, the species is currently rated as being of least concern by the International Union for Conservation of Nature due to its very large range and relatively slow rate of decline. Mexico's government lists it as endangered in that country. In 1898, it was described as being "common on the highlands of central Chiapas". However, in recent years it has become the least common of all wintering and resident warbler species in the highlands of northern Chiapas, based on point counts there.


Notes


References


Further reading


External links

Pink-headed warbler videos on the Internet Bird Collection website
Pink-headed warbler vocalizations Archived 2018-09-09 at the Wayback Machine on the Macauley Library's (Cornell Laboratory of Ornithology) website
Pink-headed warbler vocalizations on the xeno-canto.org website

The '''pink-headed warbler''' ('''''Cardellina versicolor''''') is a small [[passerine]] [[bird]] found in the southwestern highlands of [[Guatemala]] and the central and southeastern highlands of the [[Mexico|Mexican]] state of [[Chiapas]].  The adult is primarily red, with a silvery-pink head and chest.  It is a fairly common to common resident of humid to semi-humid pine-oak, pine-evergreen and evergreen forest and edge, at altitudes ranging from {{convert|1800|–|3500|m|ft|abbr=on}} [[above sea level]].

==Taxonomy==
When [[Osbert Salvin]] first described the pink-headed warbler in 1864, he assigned it to the [[genus]] ''[[Cardellina]]''.  It was also briefly assigned to ''[[Setophaga]]'', the genus of the [[American redstart]], before being moved to the genus ''[[Ergaticus]]'' in 1881.<ref>{{cite book |title=The birds of North and Middle America |first1=Robert |last1=Ridgway |first2=Herbert |last2=Friedmann |year=1901 |publisher=Government Publishing Office |location=Washington, D.C. |pages=759–760 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WYgaAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA760 |isbn=978-1-112-57414-6}}</ref> It is [[monotypic]] across its limited range, but forms a [[superspecies]] with the [[red warbler]], which is found in the highlands of Mexico, north of the [[Isthmus of Tehuantepec]].  Despite disjunct populations and considerably different plumages, the two have sometimes been considered to be [[conspecific]].<ref name=Helm>{{cite book |title=New World Warblers |first1=John |last1=Curson |first2=David |last2=Quinn |first3=David |last3=Beadle |pages=192–3 |isbn=978-0-7136-3932-2 |publisher=Christopher Helm |year=1994 |location=London}}</ref>

A comprehensive 2010 paper by Irby Lovette and colleagues analysing mitochondrial and nuclear DNA of the wood-warblers found that the red and pink-headed warblers were each other's closest relative and that their common ancestor diverged from a lineage that gave rise to the red-faced warbler. The authors recommended moving the red and pink-headed warblers back to the genus ''Cardellina'',<ref name="Lovette2010">{{cite journal | last1=Lovette | first1=Irby J. | first2=Jorge L. | last2=Pérez-Emán | first3=John P. | last3=Sullivan | first4=Richard C. | last4=Banks | first5=Isabella | last5=Fiorentino | first6=Sergio | last6=Córdoba-Córdoba | first7=María | last7=Echeverry-Galvis | first8=F. Keith | last8=Barker | first9=Kevin J. | last9=Burns | first10=John | last10=Klicka | first11=Scott M. | last11=Lanyon | first12=Eldredge | last12=Bermingham | year=2010 | title=A comprehensive multilocus phylogeny for the wood-warblers and a revised classification of the Parulidae (Aves ) | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | volume=57 | issue=2 | pages=753–770 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2010.07.018 | url=http://argentavis.org/2012/referencias/documento_89.pdf | pmid=20696258 | access-date=2018-05-02 | archive-date=2017-08-10 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170810121438/http://www.argentavis.org/2012/referencias/documento_89.pdf | url-status=usurped }}</ref> which has been adopted by the [[International Ornithologists' Union]] (IOC).<ref name="IOClist">{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | year=2018 | title= New World warblers, mitrospingid tanagers | work=World Bird List Version 8.1 | url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/warblers/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=2 May 2018}}</ref>

"Pink-headed warbler" has been designated the official name by the IOC,<ref name="IOClist"/> and is a reference to its most notable feature. The [[genus]] name ''Cardellina'' is the diminutive of the Italian {{Lang|it|cardella}}, a regional name for the [[European goldfinch]],<ref>{{cite book | last = Jobling | first = James A. | year = 2010 | title = Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | url = https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling | publisher = Christopher Helm | location= London, UK | page = [https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling/page/n91 91] | isbn = 978-1-4081-2501-4}}</ref> while its [[specific name (zoology)|specific name]], {{Lang|la|versicolor}}, is [[Latin]] for 'of changeable or various colors'.<ref>{{cite book |title=An Elementary Latin Dictionary |first=Charlton Thomas |last=Lewis |page=[https://archive.org/details/bub_gb_2ncKAAAAIAAJ/page/n915 911] |publisher=American Book Company |location=New York |year=1915 |url=https://archive.org/details/bub_gb_2ncKAAAAIAAJ}}</ref> The latter is a reference to the warbler's changeable head color, which, depending on the angle of the viewer, looks either frosty pink or a deeper red than the rest of its body.<ref name=PCA31>{{cite journal |journal=Pacific Coast Avifauna |first=Alexander F. |last=Skutch |pages=339–345 |title=Life Histories of Central American Birds |date= 1954 |format=PDF |url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/125|volume=31}}</ref> In Spanish, the species is called {{Lang|es|cabeza plateada}} or 'silvery head'.<ref name=PCA33/>

==Description==
[[File:CardellinaVersicolorWolf.jpg|thumb|Illustration by [[Joseph Wolf]] (1863)]]
The pink-headed warbler measures {{convert|12.5|–|13.5|cm|in|abbr=on}} in length,<ref name=Howell/>{{#tag:ref|By convention, length is measured from the tip of the bill to the tip of the tail on a dead bird (or skin) laid on its back.<ref>{{cite book |title=Handbook of the Birds of Europe, the Middle East and North Africa: [[Birds of the Western Palearctic]], Volume 1, Ostrich to Ducks |publisher=Oxford University Press |year=1977 |isbn=978-0-19-857358-6 |editor=Cramp, Stanley |page=3}}</ref>|group="nb"}} and weighs {{convert|10|g|oz|abbr=on}}.  Both sexes have a similar [[plumage]], though females are, on average, slightly duller overall.<ref name=Helm/> The adult has dark red upperparts, a silvery-pink chest and pinkish-red underparts.  Its head is silvery-pink, with a reddish forehead, dusky lores<ref name=Howell/> and dark brown irises.<ref>{{cite book |title=Catalogue of a Collection of Birds from Guatemala |first=Ned |last=Dearborn |page=[https://archive.org/details/bub_gb_DlUaAAAAYAAJ/page/n67 131] |year=1907 |location=Chicago |publisher=Field Museum of Natural History |url=https://archive.org/details/bub_gb_DlUaAAAAYAAJ}}</ref>  Its bill is blackish, sometimes showing some horn color on the lower mandible, and its legs are flesh-colored.<ref name=Howell/>

The juvenile is a rich brown with slightly paler underparts.<ref name=Howell/>  However, that plumage is quickly [[molt]]ed. By late summer, young birds are virtually indistinguishable from adults; only their [[ossified|unossified]] [[skull]]s distinguish them.<ref>{{cite journal |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/condor/v064n02/p0140-p0153.pdf |journal= The Condor |volume=64 |issue=2 |pages=140–153 |title=The Avifauna of the Soloma Region in Huehuetenango, Guatemala |first=Donald H. |last=Baepler |date=March 1962 |doi=10.2307/1365483|jstor= 1365483 |hdl=11244/1447 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>

===Voice===
The pink-headed warbler's call is high, thin, and somewhat metallic, variously transcribed as ''{{Not a typo|tsiu, ssing}}'' or ''{{Not a typo|tseeip}}''.<ref name=Helm/><ref name=Howell/> It also has a low, weak ''chip'' that it uses to stay in contact with its mate.<ref name="PCA31"/> Its song is a series of short trills and chips,<ref name=Howell/> described as being "clear and cheery" and reminiscent of the song of the [[yellow warbler]],<ref name="PCA31"/> as well as slower and less varied than that of the red warbler.<ref name=Helm/>  Only males sing.<ref name="PCA31"/> While its calls are heard year-round, the pink-headed warbler sings mostly between February and May, and is silent during the rainy month of June.<ref name="PCA31"/>

==Distribution and habitat==
The pink-headed warbler is [[endemism|endemic]] to the highlands of central and eastern [[Chiapas]] in Mexico, and to western Guatemala.<ref name=PCA33>{{cite journal |journal=Pacific Coast Avifauna |volume=33 |url=https://sora.unm.edu/node/127 |page=276 |title=Distributional Check-List of the Birds of Mexico, part 2 |date=20 December 1957 |publisher=Cooper Ornithological Society |editor-last=Miller |editor-first=Alden H.}}</ref>  It is a fairly common to common resident of humid to semi-humid pine-oak, pine-evergreen and evergreen forest and edge, at altitudes ranging from {{convert|1800|–|3500|m|ft|abbr=on}} above sea level.<ref name=Howell/>  It prefers forest with dense, undisturbed [[understory]], but is also found in disturbed forest with damaged understory in its strongholds in Guatemala; this is thought to be suboptimal habitat.<ref name=Helm/> Though it is among the species expected to occur in [[El Salvador]], it has not yet been reported there.<ref>{{cite journal |journal=The Wilson Bulletin |first=Oliver |last=Komar |title=Avian Diversity in El Salvador |volume=110 |issue=4 |pages=511–533 |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wilson/v110n04/p0511-p0533.pdf |date=December 1998}}</ref>

==Behavior==
Except when they are feeding fledged young, it is uncommon to find more than two pink-headed warblers together.  Mated pairs typically remain together year-round.<ref name="PCA31"/>

===Food and feeding===
Like other [[New World warbler]]s, the pink-headed warbler is an [[insectivore]], [[gleaning (birds)|gleaning]] insects and other [[invertebrate]]s from vegetation (primarily in dense understory)<ref name=Helm/> and making [[hawking (birds)|aerial sallies]] after flying prey.<ref name=Cotinga8>{{cite journal |journal=Cotinga |url=https://www.neotropicalbirdclub.org/articles/8/C8-PIWA.pdf |title=The Current Status of the Pink-headed Warbler ''Ergaticus versicolor'' in Chiapas, Mexico |volume=9 |first1=Jared |last1=Wilson |first2=Tom |last2=Will |year=1997 }}</ref> It typically forages between {{convert|2|–|5|m|ft|abbr=on}} off the ground, only seldom foraging above {{convert|7|m|ft|abbr=on}} — except during the breeding season, when the male may hunt near the tops of trees from which it sings, as high as {{convert|15|m|ft|abbr=on}} up.<ref name=Cotinga8/> The species often joins [[mixed species flock]]s that pass through its territory.<ref name=Helm/> There is some evidence that volcanic eruptions, which can coat vegetation with thick ash and impact insect populations, cause declines in pink-headed warbler numbers.<ref name=Howell>{{cite book |title=A Guide to the Birds of Mexico and Northern Central America |first1=Steve N.G |last1=Howell |first2=Sophie |last2=Webb |publisher=Oxford University Press |year=1995 |isbn=978-0-19-854012-0 |page=[https://archive.org/details/guidetobirdsofme0000howe/page/655 655] |url=https://archive.org/details/guidetobirdsofme0000howe/page/655 }}</ref><ref name=Cotinga8/>

===Breeding===
In the spring, the male begins singing on good weather days in early February and continues for the next several months, with song frequency peaking between March and May.<ref name="PCA31"/>  The female makes a globular [[bird nest|nest]] from [[pine needle]]s, building it on the ground in an area with a significant layer of fallen pine needles, often on a steep bank.  She collects pine needles more than {{convert|50|ft|m|abbr=on}} away from the nest's location, checking carefully for potential predators before returning to the nest site.<ref name="PCA31"/>  Once the exterior is completed, she fills the bottom of the nest with soft fibrous materials<ref name="PCA31"/> and lines it with moss.<ref name="Helm"/>  She lays 2–4 [[egg (biology)|eggs]],<ref name=Howell/> which are white with a wreath of pale brown spots at the large end and a sparse speckling of pale brown spots elsewhere.  The eggs average {{convert|17.1|x|13.3|mm|in|abbr=on}} in size.<ref name="PCA31"/> The female alone [[avian incubation|incubates]] for 16 days,<ref>{{cite journal |journal=The Wilson Bulletin |volume=57 |issue=4 |pages=217–242 |first=Alexander F |last=Skutch |title=Studies of Central American Redstarts |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wilson/v057n04/p0217-p0242.pdf |date=December 1945}}</ref> sitting within the domed nest with her tail sticking out of the opening and her head turned so she can see out.  She is restless while incubating, regularly changing her position.<ref name="PCA31"/>  All told, she spends some 71 percent of her time on the nest during incubation, in stints of 13–35 minutes (average 20.1 minutes) with breaks of  4–13 minutes (average 8.3 minutes).<ref>{{cite journal |journal=The Wilson Bulletin |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wilson/v074n02/p0115-p0152.pdf |volume=74 |issue=2 |pages=115–152 |title=The Constancy of Incubation |first=Alexander F |last=Skutch |date=June 1962}}</ref> Unlike many ground-nesting species, the pink-headed warbler has no [[distraction display]].<ref name="PCA31"/>

The young pink-headed warbler has a red mouth lining, a feature which has been used by taxonomists working to unravel warbler systematics.<ref>{{cite journal |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wilson/v077n01/p0071-p0075.pdf |volume=77 |issue=1 |pages=71–75 |journal=The Wilson Bulletin |first=Millicent S. |last=Ficken |title=Mouth Color of Nestling Passerines and Its Use in Taxonomy |date=March 1965 }}</ref> Nestlings spend some 10–12 days in the nest before [[fledging]].  They begin [[pair bond]]ing by their first autumn.<ref name="PCA31"/>

==Conservation and threats==
Across its range, pink-headed warbler numbers are declining, primarily because the cloud forest upon which it depends is becoming increasingly [[habitat fragmentation|fragmented]].<ref name="iucn"/> However, the species is currently rated as being of [[least concern]] by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]] due to its very large range and relatively slow rate of decline.<ref name="iucn"/> Mexico's government lists it as [[endangered species|endangered]] in that country.<ref>{{cite book |title=Birder's Conservation Handbook: 100 North American Birds at Risk |first=Jeffrey Vance |last=Wells |publisher=Princeton University Press |location=Princeton |year=2007 |isbn=978-0-691-12323-3 |page=430}}</ref> In 1898, it was described as being "common on the highlands of central Chiapas".<ref>{{cite journal |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v015n02/p0155-p0161.pdf |journal=The Auk |volume=15 |issue=2 |pages=155–161 |title=Notes on Certain Species of Mexican Birds |date=1898 |first=E. W |last=Nelson |doi=10.2307/4068244|jstor=4068244 }}</ref> However, in recent years it has become the least common of all wintering and resident warbler species in the highlands of northern Chiapas, based on point counts there.<ref>{{cite journal |journal=The Condor |volume=96 |issue=3 |pages=684–691 |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/condor/v096n03/p0684-p0691.pdf |title=The Occurrence and Ecology of the Golden-cheeked Warbler in the Highlands of Northern Chiapas, Mexico |first1=Rosa María |last1=Vidal |first2=Claudia |last2=Macías-Caballero |first3=Charles D |last3=Duncan |doi=10.2307/1369471 |year=1994|jstor=1369471 }}</ref>
